title: Ability Checks
summary: When outcome is uncertain, the DM calls for an ability check: roll d20 + ability modifier (+ proficiency if applicable).
content:
## Ability Checks

An ability check tests a character's or monster's innate talent and training in an effort to overcome a challenge.

### How It Works
1. The DM determines which of the six abilities is relevant.
2. The DM sets a **Difficulty Class (DC)** based on how hard the task is.
3. You roll a **d20** and add the relevant **ability modifier** (and **proficiency bonus** if you're proficient in a relevant skill).
4. If the total **equals or exceeds** the DC, the check succeeds.

### Difficulty Classes
| DC | Difficulty |
|---|---|
| 5 | Very Easy |
| 10 | Easy |
| 15 | Medium |
| 20 | Hard |
| 25 | Very Hard |
| 30 | Nearly Impossible |

### Skills
Each ability has associated skills:
- **Strength**: Athletics
- **Dexterity**: Acrobatics, Sleight of Hand, Stealth
- **Constitution**: (no skills, but used for concentration checks)
- **Intelligence**: Arcana, History, Investigation, Nature, Religion
- **Wisdom**: Animal Handling, Insight, Medicine, Perception, Survival
- **Charisma**: Deception, Intimidation, Performance, Persuasion

### Contests
When two creatures are competing directly, they make opposing ability checks. The higher result wins, with ties going to the defender or the status quo.

### Passive Checks
A passive check is 10 + all modifiers that normally apply. Used when the DM doesn't want to call for a roll (e.g., Passive Perception to notice hidden creatures).

title: Saving Throws
summary: A saving throw represents an attempt to resist a spell, trap, poison, disease, or similar threat. Roll d20 + ability modifier + proficiency (if proficient).
content:
## Saving Throws

A saving throw — also called a save — represents an attempt to resist a spell, a trap, a poison, a disease, or a similar threat.

### How It Works
1. Something forces you to make a save (a spell, trap, monster ability, etc.).
2. The effect specifies which ability to use (STR, DEX, CON, INT, WIS, or CHA).
3. Roll **d20 + ability modifier** (+ **proficiency bonus** if proficient in that save).
4. Meet or beat the **DC** to resist or reduce the effect.

### Class Save Proficiencies
Each class grants proficiency in two saving throws:
- **Barbarian**: Strength, Constitution
- **Bard**: Dexterity, Charisma
- **Cleric**: Wisdom, Charisma
- **Druid**: Intelligence, Wisdom
- **Fighter**: Strength, Constitution
- **Monk**: Strength, Dexterity
- **Paladin**: Wisdom, Charisma
- **Ranger**: Strength, Dexterity
- **Rogue**: Dexterity, Intelligence
- **Sorcerer**: Constitution, Charisma
- **Warlock**: Wisdom, Charisma
- **Wizard**: Intelligence, Wisdom

### Common Save Types
- **DEX saves**: Dodging area effects (Fireball, Lightning Bolt, traps)
- **CON saves**: Resisting poison, disease, maintaining concentration
- **WIS saves**: Resisting charm, fear, and mind-affecting effects
- **STR saves**: Resisting being pushed, restrained, or knocked prone
- **INT saves**: Rare; resisting psionic attacks, some illusions
- **CHA saves**: Resisting banishment, some possession effects

title: Advantage and Disadvantage
summary: Advantage means rolling two d20s and taking the higher. Disadvantage means taking the lower. They cancel each other out.
content:
## Advantage and Disadvantage

The most common ways the d20 roll is modified.

### Advantage
- Roll **two d20s** and use the **higher** result.
- Represents a favorable circumstance.

### Disadvantage
- Roll **two d20s** and use the **lower** result.
- Represents an unfavorable circumstance.

### Key Rules
- If you have **both** advantage and disadvantage, they **cancel out** — roll normally (one d20). This is true regardless of how many sources of each you have.
- Multiple sources of advantage don't stack — you still only roll two dice.
- You can't benefit from advantage on a roll if something in the situation gives you disadvantage, and vice versa.

### Common Sources of Advantage
- Attacking a prone target in melee
- Attacking while invisible
- Attacking a restrained, stunned, paralyzed, or unconscious target
- Help action (ally assists you)
- Using Inspiration
- Reckless Attack (Barbarian)
- Flanking (optional rule)

### Common Sources of Disadvantage
- Attacking a prone target at range
- Attacking while blinded, frightened, or poisoned
- Attacking at long range
- Attacking in heavy obscurement
- Wearing armor you're not proficient with (on all STR/DEX checks and saves)

title: Combat: Your Turn
summary: On your turn, you can move up to your speed, take one action, and possibly a bonus action. You also get one reaction per round.
content:
## Combat: Your Turn

Combat is organized into rounds and turns. On your turn, you can do the following in any order:

### Movement
- Move up to your **speed** in feet.
- You can break up movement before and after your action.
- Moving through difficult terrain costs 2 feet per 1 foot moved.
- You can move through a friendly creature's space, but not an enemy's (unless they're two sizes larger/smaller).

### Action
You get **one action** per turn. Common actions:
- **Attack**: Make one melee or ranged attack (more with Extra Attack).
- **Cast a Spell**: Cast a spell with a casting time of 1 action.
- **Dash**: Double your movement for the turn.
- **Disengage**: Your movement doesn't provoke opportunity attacks this turn.
- **Dodge**: Attacks against you have disadvantage; you have advantage on DEX saves.
- **Help**: Give an ally advantage on their next check or attack against a target within 5 ft.
- **Hide**: Make a Stealth check to become hidden.
- **Ready**: Prepare an action to trigger on a specific condition (uses your reaction).
- **Search**: Make a Perception or Investigation check.
- **Use an Object**: Interact with a second object or use a complex item.

### Bonus Action
- You only get a bonus action if a feature or spell specifically grants one.
- You can only take **one** bonus action per turn.
- Examples: offhand attack (two-weapon fighting), *Healing Word*, Cunning Action (Rogue).

### Free Object Interaction
- One free interaction per turn: draw/sheathe a weapon, open a door, pick up an item.

### Reaction
- **One reaction per round** (recharges at the start of your next turn).
- Triggered by a specific event.
- Most common: **Opportunity Attack** (enemy leaves your reach without Disengaging).

title: Combat: Making an Attack
summary: Roll d20 + ability modifier + proficiency bonus. If the total meets or exceeds the target's AC, the attack hits.
content:
## Making an Attack

### Attack Roll
1. Roll a **d20**.
2. Add your **ability modifier** (STR for melee, DEX for ranged, or DEX for finesse weapons).
3. Add your **proficiency bonus** if you're proficient with the weapon.
4. Compare to the target's **Armor Class (AC)**.
5. If the total **equals or exceeds** the AC, you **hit**.

### Damage Roll
On a hit, roll the weapon's **damage dice** and add the relevant **ability modifier** (same one used for the attack).

### Critical Hits
- A **natural 20** (rolling 20 on the d20) always hits, regardless of AC.
- Roll all damage dice **twice** (then add modifiers once).

### Critical Misses
- A **natural 1** (rolling 1 on the d20) always misses, regardless of modifiers.

### Melee vs. Ranged
- **Melee attacks**: Use STR modifier (or DEX with finesse weapons). Standard reach is 5 feet.
- **Ranged attacks**: Use DEX modifier. Has a normal range and a long range (disadvantage). Disadvantage if an enemy is within 5 feet of you.

### Two-Weapon Fighting
- When you Attack with a **light** melee weapon, you can use a **bonus action** to attack with a different light melee weapon in the other hand.
- You don't add your ability modifier to the damage of the bonus attack (unless it's negative), unless you have the Two-Weapon Fighting fighting style.

### Spellcasting Attacks
- Some spells require attack rolls (e.g., *Fire Bolt*, *Guiding Bolt*).
- Roll d20 + spellcasting ability modifier + proficiency bonus.
- Compare to target's AC.

title: Combat: Damage and Healing
summary: Damage reduces HP. At 0 HP, you fall unconscious and start making death saves. Healing restores HP.
content:
## Damage and Healing

### Taking Damage
- Damage reduces your **current hit points**.
- If you have **temporary hit points**, damage reduces those first.
- Damage types (fire, slashing, etc.) matter for resistances and vulnerabilities.

### Damage Resistance and Vulnerability
- **Resistance**: Take **half damage** (rounded down) from that type.
- **Vulnerability**: Take **double damage** from that type.
- **Immunity**: Take **no damage** from that type.

### Dropping to 0 Hit Points
- If damage reduces you to 0 HP, you either die outright or fall **unconscious** and start making **death saving throws**.
- **Instant Death**: If remaining damage after hitting 0 equals or exceeds your HP maximum, you die instantly. (e.g., if you have 12 max HP and take 24 damage while at 5 HP, the remaining 19 exceeds 12 — instant death.)

### Death Saving Throws
- At the start of each turn while at 0 HP, roll a **d20** (no modifiers).
- **10 or higher**: Success. **9 or lower**: Failure.
- **Three successes**: You stabilize (unconscious but not dying).
- **Three failures**: You **die**.
- **Natural 20**: Regain 1 HP and wake up.
- **Natural 1**: Counts as **two failures**.
- Taking damage while at 0 HP = one automatic failure (two if critical).

### Healing
- Healing restores hit points. You can't exceed your maximum.
- Any healing on a creature at 0 HP brings them back to consciousness.
- *Healing Word* (bonus action, ranged) is popular for reviving unconscious allies.

### Temporary Hit Points
- A buffer that absorbs damage first.
- Don't stack — take the higher value if you gain more.
- Disappear after a long rest.

title: Resting: Short and Long Rests
summary: Short rests (1 hour) let you spend hit dice. Long rests (8 hours) restore all HP, spell slots, and most resources.
content:
## Resting

### Short Rest
- At least **1 hour** of downtime (eating, reading, tending wounds).
- You can spend **hit dice** to recover HP: roll each die + CON modifier.
- Some class features recover:
  - **Warlock**: All spell slots
  - **Fighter**: Second Wind, Action Surge
  - **Monk**: All Ki points
  - **Bard**: Bardic Inspiration (at 5th level)
  - **Cleric (some domains)**: Channel Divinity
  - **Druid**: Wild Shape uses

### Long Rest
- At least **8 hours**, with no more than 2 hours of light activity (keeping watch, reading).
- You can only benefit from **one long rest per 24 hours**.
- Restores:
  - **All hit points**
  - **All spell slots**
  - **Most class features** (Action Surge, Ki, Rage, Wild Shape, Channel Divinity, etc.)
  - **Half your total hit dice** (minimum 1), rounded up
  - **Reduces exhaustion** by 1 level (with food and water)

### Hit Dice Recovery
- You regain spent hit dice equal to **half your total** (minimum 1) after a long rest.
- Example: A level 6 character regains 3 hit dice on a long rest.

title: Spellcasting Rules
summary: Spells cost spell slots to cast, have components (V/S/M), and may require concentration. Cantrips are free and unlimited.
content:
## Spellcasting

### Spell Slots
- Casting a spell of 1st level or higher uses a **spell slot** of that level or higher.
- **Upcasting**: You can cast a spell using a higher-level slot for increased effect (if the spell says so).
- Slots are restored on a **long rest** (or short rest for Warlocks).

### Cantrips
- Level 0 spells that can be cast **at will** — no spell slot required.
- Many scale with character level (e.g., *Fire Bolt* deals more damage at levels 5, 11, 17).

### Spell Components
- **V (Verbal)**: You must speak. Can't cast if silenced.
- **S (Somatic)**: You must gesture with a free hand.
- **M (Material)**: You need specific materials. A component pouch or spellcasting focus can substitute for materials without a gold cost.

### Concentration
- Some spells require **concentration** to maintain.
- You can only concentrate on **one spell at a time**.
- Casting another concentration spell ends the first.
- Taking damage forces a **Constitution saving throw** (DC = 10 or half the damage, whichever is higher). Fail = spell ends.
- Being incapacitated or killed ends concentration.

### Spell Save DC
Formula: **8 + proficiency bonus + spellcasting ability modifier**

### Spell Attack Bonus
Formula: **proficiency bonus + spellcasting ability modifier**

### Spellcasting Ability by Class
| Class | Ability |
|---|---|
| Wizard | Intelligence |
| Cleric, Druid, Ranger | Wisdom |
| Bard, Paladin, Sorcerer, Warlock | Charisma |

### Ritual Casting
- Spells with the **ritual** tag can be cast in 10 extra minutes without using a spell slot.
- Not all classes can ritual cast (Wizards, Clerics, Druids, Bards can).

### Preparing Spells
- Some classes (Cleric, Druid, Paladin, Wizard) must **prepare** a list of spells after each long rest.
- Number prepared = ability modifier + class level (varies by class).
- Known casters (Bard, Ranger, Sorcerer, Warlock) always have their spells available.

title: Cover
summary: Walls, trees, and creatures can provide cover, granting +2 or +5 to AC and DEX saves, or total protection.
content:
## Cover

Barriers between you and an attacker can provide protection.

### Half Cover (+2)
- A target has **half cover** if an obstacle blocks at least half of its body.
- Grants **+2 to AC** and **+2 to Dexterity saving throws**.
- Examples: low wall, furniture, another creature.

### Three-Quarters Cover (+5)
- A target has **three-quarters cover** if about three-quarters of it is covered.
- Grants **+5 to AC** and **+5 to Dexterity saving throws**.
- Examples: arrow slit, thick tree trunk.

### Total Cover
- A target has **total cover** if it is completely concealed.
- **Can't be targeted directly** by an attack or spell (unless the effect goes around corners).
- Examples: behind a solid wall, inside a closed room.

title: Opportunity Attacks
summary: When a creature you can see moves out of your reach, you can use your reaction to make one melee attack against it.
content:
## Opportunity Attacks

When a hostile creature that you can see moves out of your reach, you can use your **reaction** to make **one melee attack** against that creature.

### Key Rules
- Uses your **reaction** (one per round).
- Must be a **melee** attack (not ranged, not a spell unless you have War Caster).
- Triggered by **leaving** your reach, not entering it.
- The attack interrupts the movement — if it kills the creature, it drops before leaving.

### Avoiding Opportunity Attacks
- **Disengage action**: Your movement doesn't provoke OAs for the rest of the turn.
- **Teleportation**: Doesn't provoke (e.g., *Misty Step*).
- **Forced movement**: Being pushed, pulled, or knocked away doesn't provoke.
- **War Caster feat**: Lets you cast a spell (single target, 1 action casting time) instead of making a melee attack.

### Common Mistakes
- Moving *within* an enemy's reach does NOT provoke — only *leaving* it.
- You CAN make opportunity attacks while grappling an enemy (if they somehow escape).
- Rogues can use Sneak Attack on opportunity attacks (since it's once per turn, not once per round).

title: Movement and Position
summary: On your turn, you can move a distance up to your speed. Movement can be broken up before and after actions.
content:
## Movement and Position

### Speed
- Your speed is how many feet you can move on your turn.
- Most races have **30 feet**. Dwarves and Halflings have **25 feet**.

### Breaking Up Movement
- You can move before and after your action.
- Example: Move 15 feet → Attack → Move 15 feet.

### Difficult Terrain
- Each foot of movement in difficult terrain costs **2 feet**.
- Examples: rubble, undergrowth, shallow water, ice, stairs.

### Climbing, Swimming, Crawling
- Each foot costs **2 feet** of movement (3 in difficult terrain).
- The DM may require ability checks for climbing or swimming in dangerous conditions.

### Jumping
- **Long Jump**: Running = STR score in feet. Standing = half.
- **High Jump**: Running = 3 + STR modifier in feet. Standing = half.

### Falling
- Take **1d6 bludgeoning damage per 10 feet** fallen, to a maximum of 20d6.
- You land prone.

### Squeezing
- A creature can squeeze through a space one size smaller.
- Costs 2 feet per foot moved. Disadvantage on attacks and DEX saves. Attacks against have advantage.

title: Actions in Combat
summary: A quick reference for all standard actions available to every character in combat.
content:
## Actions in Combat

### Attack
Make one melee or ranged attack. At higher levels, some classes get **Extra Attack** (2 or more attacks per Attack action).

### Cast a Spell
Cast any spell with a casting time of **1 action**. Some spells use bonus actions or reactions instead.

### Dash
Gain extra movement equal to your speed for the turn. Effectively doubles your movement.

### Disengage
Your movement doesn't provoke **opportunity attacks** for the rest of the turn.

### Dodge
- Attacks against you have **disadvantage** (if you can see the attacker).
- You have **advantage** on DEX saving throws.
- Lost if you're incapacitated or your speed drops to 0.

### Help
- Help an ally with a task: they get **advantage** on their next ability check.
- Help in combat: choose an ally and a target within 5 feet of you. The ally gets advantage on their next attack against that target.

### Hide
Make a **Dexterity (Stealth)** check. On success, you're hidden (unseen and unheard). Benefits of being hidden:
- Attacks against you can't target you directly.
- Your attacks have advantage.

### Ready
Prepare an action to trigger later. Specify the trigger and the action. Uses your **reaction** when triggered. If readying a spell, you must **concentrate** on it until the trigger.

### Search
Make a **Wisdom (Perception)** or **Intelligence (Investigation)** check to find something.

### Use an Object
Interact with an object that requires your action (using a healer's kit, drinking a potion in some rules interpretations).

title: Initiative and Turn Order
summary: At the start of combat, everyone rolls d20 + DEX modifier to determine turn order. Highest goes first.
content:
## Initiative

Initiative determines the order of turns during combat.

### Rolling Initiative
1. When combat starts, every participant rolls a **d20 + Dexterity modifier**.
2. The DM ranks everyone from highest to lowest — this is the **initiative order**.
3. Ties are broken by comparing DEX scores (higher goes first). If still tied, the DM decides.

### Key Rules
- Initiative order stays the **same for the entire combat** (unless someone uses a special ability to change it).
- You can choose to **delay** by simply not doing anything on your turn (though there's no official 'delay' action in 5e — you'd use the **Ready** action instead).
- **Surprised** creatures can't move or take actions on their first turn, and can't take reactions until the end of that turn.

### Surprise
- If one side catches the other off guard, the surprised creatures are **surprised** for the first round.
- A surprised creature still rolls initiative and takes a turn — they just can't do anything on it.
- After the first round, surprise ends. Even if a surprised creature hasn't acted yet, it can now take reactions.

title: The Six Ability Scores
summary: Every character has six abilities: Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma.
content:
## The Six Ability Scores

Every creature in D&D has six abilities that describe their physical and mental characteristics.

### Strength (STR)
- Physical power and athleticism
- Used for: melee attack/damage, Athletics checks, carrying capacity, STR saves
- Important for: Fighter, Barbarian, Paladin

### Dexterity (DEX)
- Agility, reflexes, and balance
- Used for: ranged attacks, AC (unarmored/light armor), initiative, Acrobatics, Sleight of Hand, Stealth, DEX saves
- Important for: Rogue, Ranger, Monk

### Constitution (CON)
- Endurance, stamina, and health
- Used for: hit point maximum, concentration checks, CON saves
- Important for: **Everyone** — more HP is always good

### Intelligence (INT)
- Reasoning, memory, and analytical skill
- Used for: Arcana, History, Investigation, Nature, Religion, INT saves, Wizard spellcasting
- Important for: Wizard, Artificer

### Wisdom (WIS)
- Perception, intuition, and common sense
- Used for: Animal Handling, Insight, Medicine, Perception, Survival, WIS saves, Cleric/Druid/Ranger spellcasting
- Important for: Cleric, Druid, Ranger, Monk

### Charisma (CHA)
- Force of personality, persuasiveness, and presence
- Used for: Deception, Intimidation, Performance, Persuasion, CHA saves, Bard/Paladin/Sorcerer/Warlock spellcasting
- Important for: Bard, Paladin, Sorcerer, Warlock

### Modifiers
The modifier is derived from the score:

| Score | Modifier |
|---|---|
| 1 | -5 |
| 2-3 | -4 |
| 4-5 | -3 |
| 6-7 | -2 |
| 8-9 | -1 |
| 10-11 | 0 |
| 12-13 | +1 |
| 14-15 | +2 |
| 16-17 | +3 |
| 18-19 | +4 |
| 20 | +5 |

Formula: **(score - 10) ÷ 2**, rounded down.

title: Proficiency Bonus
summary: A bonus added to d20 rolls you're trained in. Starts at +2 and scales to +6 by level 17.
content:
## Proficiency Bonus

Your proficiency bonus is added to rolls for things your character is trained in.

### Scaling by Level
| Character Level | Proficiency Bonus |
|---|---|
| 1–4 | +2 |
| 5–8 | +3 |
| 9–12 | +4 |
| 13–16 | +5 |
| 17–20 | +6 |

### Where It Applies
- **Attack rolls** with weapons you're proficient in
- **Saving throws** you're proficient in
- **Skill checks** for skills you're proficient in
- **Spell attack rolls** and **spell save DC**
- **Tool checks** with tools you're proficient in

### Expertise (Double Proficiency)
Some features grant **expertise**, which means you add your proficiency bonus **twice** (e.g., a level 5 Rogue with Expertise in Stealth adds +6 instead of +3).

### You Never Add It Twice
- If a feature says to add your proficiency bonus and you already add it, you don't add it again (unless the feature says "double" / expertise).
- If multiple features would add your proficiency bonus to the same roll, you still only add it once.

title: Armor Class (AC)
summary: AC determines how hard you are to hit. Unarmored AC = 10 + DEX modifier. Armor, shields, and spells can increase it.
content:
## Armor Class (AC)

Armor Class represents how hard a creature is to hit with an attack.

### Calculating AC

**Unarmored**: 10 + DEX modifier

| Armor Type | AC Formula | Stealth |
|---|---|---|
| **Light Armor** | | |
| Padded | 11 + DEX mod | Disadvantage |
| Leather | 11 + DEX mod | — |
| Studded Leather | 12 + DEX mod | — |
| **Medium Armor** | | |
| Hide | 12 + DEX mod (max 2) | — |
| Chain Shirt | 13 + DEX mod (max 2) | — |
| Scale Mail | 14 + DEX mod (max 2) | Disadvantage |
| Breastplate | 14 + DEX mod (max 2) | — |
| Half Plate | 15 + DEX mod (max 2) | Disadvantage |
| **Heavy Armor** | | |
| Ring Mail | 14 | Disadvantage |
| Chain Mail | 16 | Disadvantage |
| Splint | 17 | Disadvantage |
| Plate | 18 | Disadvantage |

**Shield**: +2 AC (requires one free hand)

### Special AC Calculations
- **Barbarian (Unarmored Defense)**: 10 + DEX mod + CON mod
- **Monk (Unarmored Defense)**: 10 + DEX mod + WIS mod
- **Draconic Sorcerer**: 13 + DEX mod (when unarmored)
- ***Mage Armor* spell**: 13 + DEX mod
- ***Shield* spell**: +5 AC until start of next turn (reaction)

### Important Notes
- AC formulas **don't stack** — you use one calculation only.
- You must be **proficient** with armor to wear it effectively. Without proficiency: disadvantage on attacks, saves, and checks using STR or DEX, and you can't cast spells.

title: Grappling and Shoving
summary: You can use the Attack action to grapple (grab) or shove (knock prone or push) a creature using contested Athletics checks.
content:
## Grappling and Shoving

### Grappling
When you want to grab a creature:
1. Use the **Attack action** (replaces one of your attacks if you have Extra Attack).
2. The target must be **no more than one size larger** than you and within reach.
3. Make a **Strength (Athletics)** check contested by the target's **Strength (Athletics)** or **Dexterity (Acrobatics)** check (target's choice).
4. If you succeed, the target is **grappled** (speed = 0).

**While Grappling:**
- You can **drag** the grappled creature, but your speed is halved.
- You can release as a free action.
- The grapple ends if you're **incapacitated** or the target is moved out of reach.

**Escaping:**
- The grappled creature uses its **action** to make a STR (Athletics) or DEX (Acrobatics) check vs your STR (Athletics).

### Shoving
When you want to knock a creature prone or push it away:
1. Use the **Attack action** (replaces one attack).
2. Target must be no more than one size larger and within reach.
3. Make a **Strength (Athletics)** check contested by the target's **Strength (Athletics)** or **Dexterity (Acrobatics)**.
4. If you succeed, choose: **knock prone** or **push 5 feet** away.

### Tactical Combos
- **Grapple + Shove Prone**: Grapple first (they can't stand up because speed = 0), then shove prone. Now all melee attacks against them have advantage and they can't escape by standing.

title: Damage Types
summary: D&D has 13 damage types. Each type has no special rules on its own, but creatures may resist, be vulnerable, or be immune to specific types.
content:
## Damage Types

Damage types have no rules of their own, but other rules (like resistance) reference them.

### Physical Damage
- **Bludgeoning**: Blunt force — maces, hammers, falling, constriction.
- **Piercing**: Puncturing — arrows, rapiers, bite attacks.
- **Slashing**: Cutting — swords, axes, claws.

### Elemental Damage
- **Acid**: Corrosive substances — *Acid Splash*, black dragon breath.
- **Cold**: Freezing — *Ray of Frost*, white dragon breath, ice storms.
- **Fire**: Flames and heat — *Fireball*, *Burning Hands*, red dragon breath.
- **Lightning**: Electrical — *Lightning Bolt*, *Call Lightning*, blue dragon breath.
- **Thunder**: Sonic booms — *Thunderwave*, *Shatter*.

### Other Damage
- **Force**: Pure magical energy — *Magic Missile*, *Eldritch Blast*. Very few creatures resist it.
- **Necrotic**: Life-draining energy — *Blight*, *Inflict Wounds*, undead attacks.
- **Poison**: Toxic substances — *Poison Spray*, venomous bites, poisoned weapons.
- **Psychic**: Mental harm — *Vicious Mockery*, mind flayer attacks.
- **Radiant**: Divine/holy light — *Guiding Bolt*, *Sacred Flame*, angelic attacks.

### Resistance Notes
- Many undead resist or are immune to **necrotic** and **poison**.
- **Force** damage is the hardest type to resist — almost nothing is immune.
- **Magical** weapons bypass the resistance many monsters have to nonmagical bludgeoning/piercing/slashing.
